content.plain_english: This week your child is recognising coins and notes, counting amounts in pounds and pence, and finding different combinations of coins that make the same total. They are also solving small change problems.
content.in_school: Children handle real or plastic coins, sorting them and counting the totals. They make the same amount in different ways — for example, 50 p as one coin, or as two 20 p plus a 10 p. They then move on to written addition and subtraction with money, including giving change.
content.home_activity: Empty a small handful of coins onto the table. Ask your child to find three different ways to make 50 p. Then pretend they are buying something for 35 p with a 50 p coin — how much change do they get? Five minutes, no preparation needed.
dinner_table_questions: If a chocolate bar costs 65 p and you pay with a £1 coin, how much change should you get?
What two coins together make 30 p?
Can you make £1 using only silver coins? Using only copper coins?

key_vocabulary.definition: The smaller unit of UK money. 100 p makes £1.
The larger unit of UK money. £1 is the same as 100 p.
The full amount when you add the value of coins or notes together.
The money you get back when you pay more than the price of an item.
A round piece of metal money. UK coins are 1 p, 2 p, 5 p, 10 p, 20 p, 50 p, £1 and £2.

example_questions: Find two different ways to make 60 p using coins.
Sam has a 20 p, a 10 p and a 5 p coin. How much does he have in total?
A book costs 80 p. Mia pays with £1. How much change does she get?
content.times_table_tip: Money is a great way to practise the 5 and 10 times tables. Counting up in 5 p coins or 10 p coins is the same as reciting those tables — try it with the coins from your wallet.
